event: 65 years old
male
treated at the Pulmonary Department
no history of smoking
no underlying pulmonary diseases
4-day history of gradually increasing shortness of breath
fever
dry cough
fatigue
reduced breath sounds
oxygen saturation (SpO2) was 93%
chest CT scans demonstrated a bilateral peripheral ground-glass attenuation and patchy consolidation
lung involvement was 60%-70%
nasopharyngeal SARS-CoV-2 RT-PCR test was positive
increased temperature remained
saturation with free breathing decreased to 90%
white cell count: 7.93 х 109/ L all (80% neutrophils, 8 % lymphocytes, and 10% monocytes, 2% plasma cell)
hemoglobin 159 g/l
platelet count: 468 × 109/l
Westergren ESR 41 mm/h
interleukine 6 102 pg/ml
С-reactive protein 142 mg/l
ferritin 939.92 μg/ml
D-dimer 609 ng/ml1
procalcitonin 0,11 ng/ml
chest radiography on Day 15 after admission showed the presence of air and pleural effusion in the right pleural cavity with collapse of the right lung
upper level of pleural fluid was located at the level of the third rib
patient was transferred to the Intensive Care Unit (ICU)
thoracentesis and thoracostomy in the 6th intercostal space on the mid-axillary line were performed
about 1400 ml of a yellowish opaque liquid was evacuated from the pleural cavity
a follow-up chest radiograph demonstrated lack of air in the right pleural cavity and the persistence of fluid remains
the lung was expanded
Linezolid and imipenem/cilastatin therapy was initiated immediately
patient continued to receive oxygen supplementation through the simple face mask with flow rate of 8-10 l/min
daily drainage volume was from 300 to 1000 ml of fluid
chest computed tomography obtained on day 3 after thoracentesis still showed pleural effusion with gas bubbles
right lung was reduced in volume by half
focal area of subpleural infiltration with a central cavity of destruction up to 6.5 mm in diameter was revealed
air layer up to 47 mm of anteroposterior thickness was revealed along the anterior chest wall
radiological sings of the left side hydropneumothorax were also found
pleural fluid analysis confirmed an exudative lymphocytic-rich effusion with no growth of acid-fast bacteria (AFB)
gram-negative bacteria as Acinetobacter baumannii and Pseudomonas aeruginosa were cultured
urine culture was positive for Klebsiella pneumonia
needle thoracocentesis and new pleural drainage in the second intercostal space on the midclavicular line was established
Air and creamy purulent mass have been aspirated
200 to 800 ml of serofibrinous hemorrhagic fluid was drawn daily through 2 drainage tubes
chest CT showed the presence of air and pleural effusion in the right hemithorax
diagnosis of pleural empyema was confirmed
patient was transferred to the Surgical Department of the 10th Minsk city clinical hospital
the right pleural space was daily irrigated with antiseptic solutions
lung expansion was achieved by continuous vacuum aspiration technique
encapsulated pleural effusion located in the upper anterior area of the right hemithorax was identified
ultrasound-guided puncture of this effusion was performed and a new drainage of the pleural cavity was installed
chest CT showed the presence of a small amount of fluid in the right pleural cavity

4-week antibiotic therapy (1 week in the Pulmonary Department and 3 weeks in the Surgical Department) course with colistimethatum natrium (1 million units 2 times a day) and imipenem/cilastatin (1 g 3 times a day)
plasma D dimer level was 2424 ng/ml
chest CT after chest tube removing showed the presence of a small amount of fluid in the right pleural cavity
Lab test scores came back to normal range
level of С-reactive protein was 11.7 mg/l, procalcitonin − < 0,1 ng/ml
